beats.prompt: Quick warm-up from last time — match each wait to its average length on a fair coin.

pairs.left: E[H] — the wait for the first heads

feedback.correct: Right — HH averages 6 flips, HT averages 4. Hold onto that.
hints: No pressure — this is just a warm-up.
Overlap made one of them wait longer.

beats.prompt: Read this key distinction — 'who's first' is not 'how long' — then continue.

interaction.title: "Who's first" is not "how long"
interaction.body: Two friends watch the same coin stream. One waits for HH, the other for HT. "How long until mine shows up?" and "whose shows up first?" are different questions — and they can have surprising answers.

beats.prompt: We both watch one coin. You wait for HT, I wait for HH. HT is faster on its own (4 vs 6) — so HT should win the race, right?

HT wins the race.note: Tempting — but "faster alone" doesn't decide a shared-stream race. Watch what actually happens.

HH wins the race.note: Let's see — the shared stream makes this less lopsided than it looks.

It's a coin-flip tie.note: Great instinct — on one shared stream HH vs HT is a dead 50/50.

hints: No wrong guess yet — commit and we'll race it.
On a shared stream, the first H sets up both.

beats.prompt: Run the race. Watch one slowly, then run a couple hundred.

hero.structuralReadout: HH and HT each win about 50 of every 100 races — a dead tie.

feedback.correct: A tie — even though HH waits longer on its own.

beats.prompt: Why a tie? After the first H lands, match each next flip to who it hands the race to.

feedback.correct: Exactly — after the first H, one fair flip decides it. 50/50.
hints: Think about the moment right after the first H.
The next flip (H or T) picks the winner.

beats.prompt: New game: you pick HHH. Going second, type the 3-flip pattern (H/T) that beats HHH most often.

feedback.correct: THH it is — it sneaks in just before HHH can complete.
hints: The opposite pattern is not the strongest counter.
You want to appear right before HHH completes.
Put a T in front: THH.

beats.prompt: Now race HHH against THH. Watch one, then run the batch.

hero.structuralReadout: THH won about 7 of every 8 races against HHH.

beats.interviewNote: This is the classic Penney's Game / second-mover edge: the exact 7:1 comes from Conway's leading numbers, and the non-arbitrage flavor makes it a trading-desk favorite.

feedback.correct: About 7 to 1 — going second is a real advantage.

beats.prompt: Read how a win-chance differs from a wait-time, then continue.

interaction.title: A chance has no per-flip cost
interaction.body: When we counted how LONG a pattern takes, every flip added 1. A win-CHANCE is different: there's no "+1" — you're just splitting probability between outcomes that end at 1 (you win) or 0 (you don't).

beats.prompt: A win-CHANCE is not a wait-TIME. Match each quantity to how its recurrence — the rule linking a state to its neighbors — behaves.

feedback.correct: Right — no "+1". A probability recurrence just splits chances and ends at 1 or 0.
hints: The "+1" belongs to duration, not probability.
Pick the shape with no +1, ending at 1·0.

beats.prompt: Tap the pattern that beats the current target. Is there a single "best" pattern?

feedback.correct: No best pattern — the "beats" relation goes in a loop.
hints: Pick the counter, then see the cycle.
Each pattern is beaten by another.

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge: your friend goes first and picks TTT. Going second, pick the 3-flip pattern that beats it most often, and give your exact win probability as a fraction.

interaction.scenario: You both watch the same fair-coin stream; whoever's pattern appears first wins. (Recall how THH was built to beat HHH.)

feedback.correct: HTT beats TTT 7 times out of 8. It's the mirror of THH-beats-HHH: take the friend's pattern, flip its second symbol, and slide it in front. TTT wins only when the first three flips are all tails (1/8); otherwise an H lands in front of a TT and completes HTT first.
hints: Don't pick the opposite (HHH) or the reverse. You want a pattern that finishes just before TTT can.
Use the second-mover rule: flip the friend's 2nd symbol and put it in front, keeping the first two. TTT becomes H + TT. For the odds, ask how TTT could ever win.
Counter = HTT. TTT wins only if the first three flips are TTT (probability 1/8); every other start lets an H slip in front of a TT, so you win 1 - 1/8 = 7/8.

beats.prompt: Reveal the recap cards to review what you showed: race ≠ wait, second-mover edge, no best pattern.

feedback.correct: Race ≠ wait; going second wins; there's no best pattern.
hints: Faster-alone can still tie or lose.
